The two options with First Republic Bank are pretty much:
=09Do something, or=09Do nothing.

=E2=80=9CDo something=E2=80=9D is obviously bad. First Republic=E2=80=99s  =
balance sheet shows about $233 billion of assets, including about $173 bill=
ion of loans, but the market value of those assets is considerably lower: T=
hose loans are largely mortgages made at very low interest rates, and they =
have lost a lot of value as rates have gone up.. First Republic=C2=A0estima=
ted as of Dec. 31 that its assets were worth about $27 billion less than th=
eir carrying value.
  [1]
=C2=A0So figure its assets are worth something like $206 billion on a good =
day.
Meanwhile it=C2=A0has about $105 billion of deposits and about $105 billion=
 of secured borrowing from the Federal Reserve and Federal Home Loan Bank s=
ystem. Of those deposits, roughly $55 billion are insured by the Federal De=
posit Insurance Corp. and roughly $50 billion aren=E2=80=99t; $30 billion o=
f the unsecured deposits belong to a consortium of big banks that=C2=A0depo=
sited money with First Republic last month to boost confidence. Roughly spe=
aking, the insured deposits and the Fed/FHLB ($160 billion total) get paid =
back first, the uninsured deposits ($50 billion) get paid back next, and ev=
erybody else =E2=80=94=C2=A0subordinated debt, shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
gets paid back with whatever is left.
So if you can sell the assets for about $210 billion, then the government a=
nd all of the depositors get paid back in full; if you can=E2=80=99t, they =
don=E2=80=99t. (Either way, the shareholders are, uh, in trouble.) Again, t=
he assets are worth something like $206 billion, based on First Republic=E2=
=80=99s filings in December; that would not quite be enough to pay everyone=
 back. But  the consensus seems to be that if you actually had to go sell e=
verything at once, things would be considerably worse, and there would be a=
 hole of tens of billions of dollars.
And so all of the do-something options are bad, because of that hole. The m=
ost straightforward do-something option is that the FDIC could seize First =
Republic, sell its assets, and use the money to pay back depositors. But th=
ere would be a hole of tens of billions of dollars. And the FDIC would eith=
er have to fill that hole (declaring First Republic systemically important =
and using its deposit insurance fund to pay off the uninsured depositors), =
or=C2=A0not=C2=A0fill that hole (letting the uninsured depositors bear the =
loss). The  Wall Street Journal notes:

The details and extent of the FDIC=E2=80=99s support will be determined on =
whether they use the same tool, a so-called systemic risk exception, that a=
llowed the agency to guarantee all of the depositors at last month=E2=80=99=
s two failed institutions.
Invoking that exception again would allow regulators to backstop all of the=
 roughly $50 billion in deposits at First Republic that are above the FDIC=
=E2=80=99s insurance limit, including the $30 billion deposited by the big =
banks.
If the FDIC doesn=E2=80=99t make those depositors whole, it could reignite =
questions about such deposits at other regional banks, causing customers to=
 yank their deposits from smaller firms. But if it does, the FDIC could be =
accused of bailing out Wall Street.

If the FDIC takes over First Republic at a loss, somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0th=
e uninsured depositors (meaning largely but not exclusively the big banks) =
or the FDIC (also meaning largely the big banks, who pay to fund the FDIC=
=E2=80=99s insurance fund) =E2=80=94=C2=A0has to bear the loss.
There are other do-something options that could happen in the shadow of an =
FDIC takeover: Another bank could buy First Republic and assume its deposit=
s, or other banks could buy its assets at above-market prices, or banks or =
private equity firms could buy some equity in First Republic. Bloomberg New=
s reports:

A number of rescue proposals have so far failed to come to fruition.=C2=A0
Earlier this week, Bloomberg reported that First Republic was looking to po=
tentially sell $50 billion to $100 billion of assets to big banks that woul=
d also receive warrants or preferred equity as an incentive to buy the hold=
ings above their market value.
By Wednesday, the firm=E2=80=99s advisers were privately pitching a similar=
 concept, in which stronger banks would buy bonds off of First Republic=E2=
=80=99s books for more than they were worth so that it could sell shares to=
 new investors. While that would mean booking initial losses, banks could h=
old the debts through repayment to be made whole.

But all of these have the same basic outcome, which is that somebody =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0probably, again, one or more big banks =E2=80=94=C2=A0steps in to =
bear the losses, to buy First Republic=E2=80=99s assets for more than they =
are worth. Nobody likes it:

The fate of First Republic Bank has become a game of chicken between the US=
 government and the lender=E2=80=99s largest rivals, with both sides seekin=
g to avoid steep losses and hoping the other will handle the troubled firm.=
 =E2=80=A6
Executives at five of the biggest banks, speaking on the condition they not=
 be named, dismissed the notion of once again banding together to prop up F=
irst Republic, especially when it could mean paving the way for investors o=
r a competitor to scoop up the firm at a bargain price.=C2=A0

If the big banks bear the losses on First Republic, then whoever ends up ow=
ning First Republic =E2=80=94=C2=A0its current shareholders, a new buyer =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t. You can finesse that a little bit with warran=
ts =E2=80=94=C2=A0effectively, you make the banks who take the losses=C2=A0=
also=C2=A0the new owners of First Republic =E2=80=94=C2=A0but the main prob=
lem doesn=E2=80=99t go away. The main problem is the losses.
The other option is =E2=80=9Cdo nothing.=E2=80=9D First Republic  reported =
earnings on Monday, and they were   legendarily awful:
Across the industry, First Republic=E2=80=99s quarterly earnings report on =
Monday has come to be regarded as a disaster. The firm announced a larger-t=
han-expected drop in deposits, then declined to take questions as executive=
s presented a 12-minute briefing on results.=C2=A0
But First Republic=C2=A0reported a profit. The   problem, for First Republi=
c, is that lots of its low-interest deposits have fled, and it has had to r=
eplace their funding by borrowing from the Fed, the FHLB and the big banks =
at much higher rates. Meanwhile it still has lots of long-term loans made a=
t low interest rates. If you borrow short at 0% to lend long at 3%, and the=
n your short-term borrowing costs go up to 5% while your loans stay the sam=
e, you will be losing 2% a year on your loans, and that is roughly the stat=
e that First Republic finds itself in. But it is=C2=A0not=C2=A0exactly=C2=
=A0the state that First Republic finds itself in: It still has some cheap i=
nsured deposits, some short-term assets, some floating-rate assets, some fe=
e income, and in fact it has managed to scrape out a profit even as rates h=
ave moved against it. Can that last? I mean,  maybe not:

The deposit run has forced First Republic to rely on other, more expensive =
funding. That makes it hard to generate interest income, and at some point =
it might not be able to.
=E2=80=9CThey=E2=80=99ve never been super profitable,=E2=80=9D said Tim Cof=
fey, managing director and analyst at Janney Montgomery Scott. =E2=80=9CNow=
 you=E2=80=99re not growing and you=E2=80=99re layering on really high borr=
owing and funding costs.=E2=80=9D

But a bank can stay in business even with some quarterly losses, as long as=
 it remains well capitalized, and as a technical matter First Republic has =
enough capital to withstand some unprofitable quarters. And if you muddle a=
long for long enough, the situation can right itself: The long-term low-int=
erest loans will roll off and be replaced with higher-interest new loans, a=
nd First Republic=E2=80=99s interest margins will start to expand again. It=
 might work! If you are a First Republic=C2=A0shareholder, =E2=80=9Cdo noth=
ing and hope the business recovers=E2=80=9D is clearly the best option.
Of course deposits might keep flowing out, but so what? First Republic is n=
ow funded in large part with loans from the Fed and the FHLB, and I suppose=
 they could just lend it some more money. When Silicon Valley Bank failed, =
  the Fed put in place a new Bank Term Funding Program that was designed fo=
r more or less this purpose: The BTFP lets banks borrow against their asset=
s without taking into account interest-rate losses, so that they can replac=
e fleeing deposits with loans from the Fed. US regional banks spent years i=
n a low interest rate environment, they were caught out by a rapid rate hik=
ing cycle, and the Fed responded to that problem by lending them money to s=
mooth out the transition.=C2=A0
The advantage of doing nothing is that nobody has to take any losses now. B=
ut the regulators seem to want to move. Bloomberg again:

The clock for striking such a deal began ticking louder late last week. US =
regulators reached out to some industry leaders, encouraging them to make a=
 renewed push to find a private solution to shore up First Republic=E2=80=
=99s balance sheet, according to people with knowledge of the discussions.=
=C2=A0
The calls also came with a warning that banks should be prepared in case so=
mething happens soon.

And one way for something to happen soon is if the Fed   stops lending to F=
irst Republic:
As weeks keep passing without a transaction, senior [FDIC] officials are in=
creasingly weighing whether to downgrade their scoring of the firm=E2=80=99=
s condition, including its so-called Camels rating, according to people wit=
h direct knowledge of the talks. That would likely limit the bank=E2=80=99s=
 use of the Fed=E2=80=99s discount window and an emergency facility launche=
d last month, the people said.
Why? Why close a bank and take billions of dollars of losses if you don=E2=
=80=99t have to? The consequences of doing something are obvious and bad; t=
he consequences of doing nothing are a bit more diffuse.
But let=E2=80=99s talk about some of them. One is that there are legal limi=
ts on the Fed=E2=80=99s ability to keep propping up First Republic.=C2=A0I =
mentioned the BTFP, the Fed=E2=80=99s post-Silicon Valley Bank program that=
 lends to banks at 100% of the=C2=A0face value=C2=A0of their collateral, ev=
en if that collateral has lost money due to rising interest rates. But only=
 US Treasury and agency securities are  eligible to be BTFP collateral, and=
 First Republic=E2=80=99s assets are mostly loans. Those loans tend to be p=
retty=C2=A0safe=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0they are mostly   mortgages to rich peo=
ple =E2=80=94=C2=A0but they are very exposed to interest-rate risk, so they=
 have lost a lot of value. And it can=E2=80=99t use them to borrow from the=
 BTFP.
Meanwhile these loans are eligible collateral at the Fed=E2=80=99s discount=
 window, its more standard lending program, but the discount window lends a=
gainst the=C2=A0market=C2=A0value of collateral, and these loans have lost =
a lot of value. If deposits keep fleeing from First Republic, its ability t=
o replace those deposits with Fed loans depends on the=C2=A0market=C2=A0val=
ue of its assets, which means it might run out of capacity. If the FDIC is =
worried about that happening sometime soon, then there is some urgency to d=
o something first.
More generally, the=C2=A0theory of central banking is that central banks sh=
ould lend to solvent banks, but not prop up insolvent banks. The Fed=E2=80=
=99s statutes limit its ability to lend to undercapitalized banks. In some =
obvious economic sense, First Republic is undercapitalized =E2=80=94=C2=A0i=
ts assets are worth less than its liabilities, which is why we are talking =
about this =E2=80=94=C2=A0but legally it is fine and has plenty of regulato=
ry capital.
But at some point, if the regulators conclude that First Republic is not vi=
able, it is at least, like, embarrassing for them to keep lending it money.=
 In the limit case, if all of First Republic=E2=80=99s deposits fled, you c=
ould imagine the Fed lending it $210 billion (up from its current $105 bill=
ion of Fed/FHLB money) so it could continue to limp along. But that=E2=80=
=99s bad! You don=E2=80=99t want a bank out there doing business, making lo=
ans, paying executive salaries, that is entirely funded by the Fed. You nee=
d some private-sector endorsement of the bank for the Fed to keep supportin=
g it.=C2=A0
Also: The losses have already happened. First Republic made loans at low in=
terest rates, now interest rates are higher, and so its loans are not worth=
 what they used to be. As an accounting matter, those losses don=E2=80=99t=
=C2=A0have=C2=A0to be recognized yet; First Republic=E2=80=99s balance shee=
t is still technically solvent, and it can muddle along for a while. But=C2=
=A0 economically the difference between =E2=80=9Cthe banking system reports=
 billions of dollars of losses today and then normal profits afterwards=E2=
=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cthe banking system bleeds these losses into lower accou=
nting profits for the next few years=E2=80=9D is not that great, and the fo=
rmer is more clarifying.

   =20
      Regulatory arbitrage
   =20
 =20

I used to be a corporate equity derivatives investment banker, which means =
that I=E2=80=99d go to companies and try to convince them to buy or sell op=
tions on their own stock. If you read a finance textbook, you will get the =
impression that derivatives are mostly about risk management, about hedging=
 and speculation: People who own stock buy put options to protect themselve=
s agains price declines, or sell call options to transform some of their po=
tential upside into cash now; people who don=E2=80=99t own stock buy calls =
to take some cheap stock-price risk, etc.=C2=A0
This describes almost none of what I was selling to these companies. What I=
 was selling, much of the time, was=C2=A0tax deductions: We could build you=
 a derivative that, sure, had the economic properties of some call options,=
 but that took advantage of technical tax rules to get you extra deductions=
.
  [2]
=C2=A0Other times I was selling=C2=A0accounting treatment:=C2=A0We could do=
 a thing that had the economic properties of buying back stock, but that to=
ok advantage of technical accounting rules to juice your earnings per share=
 a bit more.
  [3]
 Or I might be=C2=A0selling=C2=A0something like securities-law compliance: =
If you had some legal restriction on your ability to buy or sell stock, you=
 could buy a derivative from us that was economically like buying or sellin=
g stock, but that avoided those restrictions.
  [4]

In my line of work, derivatives were essentially about=C2=A0regulatory arbi=
trage:=C2=A0There were some complicated rules, created by legislators or re=
gulators or accounting standards-setters, and those rules had the effect of=
 rewarding some things and penalizing other things. And our job was to find=
 a way to take something that the rules penalized and=C2=A0turn it into som=
ething that the rules rewarded, without changing its economic substance too=
 much.
A famous example. The US tax code=C2=A0discourages short-term trading and e=
ncourages long-term investing. If you=C2=A0borrow a lot of money to rapidly=
 trade stocks, you will be penalized, paying short-term capital gains rates=
. If you buy a long-term call option on a variable basket of stocks that yo=
u have management rights over, you will be rewarded, paying long-term capit=
al gains rates. If you notice that those two things are the same, you can h=
ave a lucrative career as a derivatives structurer.=C2=A0(And get in troubl=
e: This is  the Renaissance Technologies tax trade, and it  ended up not wo=
rking.)
The lesson that I learned from my career as a derivatives structurer=C2=A0i=
s that much of finance is about this sort of regulatory arbitrage. Economic=
 life is socially constructed, society has rules, and you can make use of t=
he rules to make money.
Sometimes the rules change and particular businesses get harder. But there =
is a sort of conservation law at work; the rules for complex systems have t=
o be somewhat complex, and if you have mastered the current complexities yo=
u can probably figure out how to make money off=C2=A0new and different comp=
lexities. Tax trades sometimes get shut down by new tax rules, but that doe=
sn=E2=80=99t put tax structurers=C2=A0out of work; they=E2=80=99re the ones=
 who are best positioned to figure out the new tax trades enabled by the ne=
w rules.
Sometimes some whole new area of economic life gets brought into the domain=
 of rules, and a new industry springs up to game it. In my lifetime, enviro=
nmental, social and governance investing went from an academic idea to a hu=
ge business with all sorts of competing and economically important regulato=
ry and accounting regimes, and so there are lots of people in the financial=
 industry working on ESG arbitrage.
  [5]
=C2=A0=E2=80=9CESG Consultant But Evil,=E2=80=9D I  sometimes call this job=
, and we  talked last week about how companies whose business is cutting do=
wn trees can get environmental credit for the trees they don=E2=80=99t cut =
down. That is the purest form of financial engineering: Some accounting reg=
ime exists that rewards you for not cutting down trees, you are in the busi=
ness of cutting down trees, and you find a way to make cutting down trees l=
ook, for the relevant accounting purposes, like not cutting down trees.
More rarely, some set of rules will go away or be simplified in a way that =
really does demolish someone=E2=80=99s business niche. For instance, there =
is, in the US,  a rule saying that bank accounts are insured by the Federal=
 Deposit Insurance Corp. up to $250,000. FDIC insurance is valuable, but yo=
u cannot pay the FDIC directly for the amount of insurance that you want: I=
f you have $250,000=C2=A0in your bank account, it is all insured without yo=
u doing anything; if you have $2,500,000, it mostly isn=E2=80=99t, and that=
=E2=80=99s just that. But of course you can open 10 accounts at 10 banks an=
d put $250,000 in each of them; then they will all be insured. If you have =
$25,000,000, you can open 100 accounts at 100 banks, but that is pretty ann=
oying. But someone=C2=A0else=C2=A0can open 100 accounts for you at 100 bank=
s, and put $250,000 in each of them, and charge you a fee. That intermediar=
y is=C2=A0selling you FDIC insurance, and charging you a fee for it, becaus=
e it has found a (fairly straightforward) way to structure around this one =
FDIC rule.
And that really exists and is a business (=E2=80=9Cbrokered deposits=E2=80=
=9D). And if the FDIC tinkered with its rules in some way =E2=80=94=C2=A0if=
 it changed the rules so that all of a household=E2=80=99s=C2=A0bank accoun=
ts at one bank counted together toward the limit, or so that checking and s=
avings accounts at the same bank counted separately, or whatever =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0then probably the intermediaries who currently sell this product woul=
d be best positioned to sell a revised product to comply with the new rules=
; they are the experts at FDIC insurance cap structuring.
But if the FDIC did away with the limit entirely they=E2=80=99d be out of b=
usiness.  David Dayen=C2=A0reports:

Legislation has been proposed to uncap deposit insurance. And that has prom=
pted the private equity=E2=80=93owned company that is one of the main benef=
iciaries of the cap to spring into action.
A company called IntraFi offers two products that allow large depositors to=
 spread their money among a network of hundreds of banks, each with account=
s that don=E2=80=99t exceed the cap and are therefore effectively covered i=
n full by deposit insurance. The company takes a fee for facilitating these=
 =E2=80=9Cbrokered deposits.=E2=80=9D If deposit insurance were uncapped, t=
heir business model would be worthless.
The prospect of uncapping has the extremely well-connected officials at Int=
raFi scrambling. In the first quarter of 2023, when Silicon Valley Bank was=
 shuttered, IntraFi tripled its lobbying expenses and hired a firm known fo=
r its access to senior congressional leadership. The new lobbyists who regi=
stered to work for IntraFi have experience with senior members of Congress,=
 as well as the Trump and Obama White Houses. It=E2=80=99s a full-court pre=
ss to maintain a lucrative status quo. =E2=80=A6
Brokered deposits have been heavily criticized by former FDIC chair Sheila =
Bair, who described them as =E2=80=9Cjust gaming the FDIC rules. The FDIC t=
akes all the credit risk, and Promontory [IntraFi=E2=80=99s predecessor] ge=
ts the profit.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

Sure, yes, I don=E2=80=99t disagree, I=E2=80=99d just point out that =E2=80=
=9C[product]=C2=A0is just gaming the [government agency] rules; the governm=
ent takes all the credit risk and [company]=C2=A0gets the profit=E2=80=9D d=
escribes a surprisingly large portion of finance!

   =20
      Crypto money markets
   =20
 =20

Here is a story that you could tell about crypto in 2020 and 2021. Interest=
 rates, in the US, in traditional finance, were very low. If you had some m=
oney and you wanted to earn a yield on it, and you put it in the bank, you=
=E2=80=99d earn roughly 0% interest, and you=E2=80=99d be sad. Crypto found=
 a solution to this. The solution was that crypto platforms =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
exchanges, lending programs, etc. =E2=80=94 would take your money and lend =
it to absolutely wild leveraged degenerate crypto traders, who would pay hi=
gh interest rates on that money so they could gamble with it.
  [6]
 For a while crypto mostly went up, the gamblers mostly made money and paid=
 their interest, and you could get, like, 18% yields on bank-account-ish-lo=
oking crypto accounts. And then this system, which was as dumb as it sounds=
, collapsed, and crypto platforms like Celsius and Voyager and BlockFi went=
 bankrupt.
This system collapsed for a number of reasons, but probably one of them was=
 that the Federal Reserve raised interest rates, which reduced the value of=
 speculative assets like crypto. But there is a bright side to that, for cr=
ypto, which is that now if you are a crypto platform and you want to attrac=
t deposits by promising to pay interest, that is much easier. You can take =
your customers=E2=80=99 money and buy Treasury bills yielding 5% and pay th=
em 4% and keep the difference, instead of taking your customers=E2=80=99 mo=
ney and flinging it into crazy leveraged gambling and losing it and going b=
ankrupt. This is not, I suppose, =E2=80=9Ccrypto,=E2=80=9D in some relevant=
 sense, but you do it on the blockchain, blah blah blah, it=E2=80=99s crypt=
o enough. The  Wall Street Journal reports:

=E2=80=9CThe high-yield DeFi app era is over,=E2=80=9D said Sidney Powell, =
chief executive of decentralized lender Maple Finance.
As of Tuesday, one of the biggest DeFi platforms, Aave, was offering a 30-d=
ay deposit rate of only around 2% for the two largest stablecoins, cryptocu=
rrencies pegged to the dollar.=C2=A0=C2=A0
Maple Finance is among a host of crypto firms seizing on the higher yields =
and relative safety offered by U.S. government bonds. Last week, the compan=
y launched a product that will earn interest equivalent to a one-month Trea=
sury bill minus a fee on deposits=E2=80=94offering a yield of about 3.4%.
This is how the product works: non-U.S. accredited investors can deposit US=
D Coin into the =E2=80=9Ccash management pool=E2=80=9D and receive tokens t=
hat represent their ownership in exchange. The pool then issues a loan to a=
n entity managed by crypto hedge fund Room40 Capital, which will invest cus=
tomer deposits in one-month Treasury bills.

Is this product better than a money market fund? Well! The money market fun=
d (1) seems to pay higher interest and (2) does not involve LENDING YOUR MO=
NEY TO CRYPTO HEDGE FUND ROOM40 CAPITAL=C2=A0to buy Treasury bills. But thi=
s product does have the advantage of being cryyyyyyyyyypto:

Proponents of these new products say they appeal to big crypto investors wh=
o want to keep stablecoins on hand for fast trading and earn a return on id=
le cash.
Of course, traders can always sell their stablecoin holdings and park them =
in Treasury bills directly without taking the risk of going through a third=
 party and paying a fee.=C2=A0

But where is the fun in that. In the olden days, crypto paid higher=C2=A0yi=
elds than traditional finance, to compensate people for taking enormous amo=
unts of risk. Now it pays lower yields than traditional finance and, uh, we=
ll. To be fair,  Maple explains that =E2=80=9Cassets are held in standalone=
 single purpose vehicle, custodied by a regulated prime broker and Lenders =
have full recourse over all assets.=E2=80=9D
  [7]

Elsewhere in   investing crypto stablecoin assets in Treasury bills:

Franklin Templeton says its money-market fund that records share ownership =
on a blockchain is seeing inflows from crypto-related entities in the after=
math of the shuttering of several industry-friendly banks.=C2=A0
Total assets in the Franklin OnChain US Government Money Fund (FOBXX), whic=
h was launched in 2021 and became publicly available last year, have increa=
sed to around $270 million. The fund uses the Stellar blockchain network to=
 process transactions and record ownership. The fund invests in US governme=
nt securities, cash and repurchase agreements and doesn=E2=80=99t hold any =
cryptocurrencies. =E2=80=A6
The fund even has a digital token called BENJI that represents shares of th=
e funds. One share of the fund is maintained at one dollar. The tokens are =
currently not transferable between fund investors, but executives at the as=
set manager said that bringing utility to the token is on the roadmap of th=
e firm.

We   talked about this fund back in 2019, and I love it, though I still wis=
h that they called the token a =E2=80=9CBenjamin.=E2=80=9D One way to think=
 about the BENJI is that it is a stablecoin that is worth a dollar, lives o=
n the blockchain, is invested in safe assets by a regulated entity=C2=A0and=
 (unlike most stablecoins) pays interest. Another way to think about the BE=
NJI is that it is just a normal government money market fund, except that t=
he people promoting it say =E2=80=9Cblockchain=E2=80=9D a lot, so if you ar=
e a crypto investor you will feel better about giving it your money.=C2=A0

gages worth about $117.5 billion (against $136.7 billion carrying value), o=
ther loans worth $26.4 billion (against $29.3 billion), municipal securitie=

  [2] The key trade here is the convertible bond call spread. Here is a col=
umn I wrote about the economics of the call spread, but the important part =
is footnote 3, about the tax treatment.


  [3] Here the key trade is the accelerated share repurchase. Here is a col=

  [4] Various trades here. For a company, an ASR is one way to implement a =
10b5-1 plan to buy back stock even when you are not in an open window. For =
an activist, a total return swap is a way to economically buy stock without=
 triggering disclosure requirements. For a big shareholder, a funded collar=
 is a way to economically sell stock while still being able to say you are =
a shareholder (and having voting rights, etc.).


  [5] Also: crypto.

sing the money to earn yield by staking tokens or providing liquidity or wh=
atever, but at a high level =E2=80=94 and in terms of realized results =E2=
=80=94 they were mostly gambling. The key text here is probably Kyle Davies=
=E2=80=99s  absolutely astounding explanation of Three Arrows Capital=E2=80=
=99s =E2=80=9Carbitrage=E2=80=9D trades, which   we discussed in December. =
=E2=80=9CWe will lend money to a sophisticated market making firm to conduc=
t arbitrage trades that earn steady profits that it can use to pay off the =
loan=E2=80=9D sounds reasonable-ish, until you hear Davies describe what th=
ose arbitrage trades were.


  [7] What *is* the trade here? Here is Maple=E2=80=99s deck on the strateg=
y, but to me the hard part is that Room40 is getting USDC (a stablecoin) an=
d buying Treasury bills, presumably using dollars. It needs to convert the =
USDC into dollars, which I think means borrowing dollars secured by USDC?

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">First Republic</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The two options with First Republic Bank are p=
retty much:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Do something, or</li>=09<li style=3D"=
margin-bottom: 5px;">Do nothing.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CDo something=E2=80=9D is obviously ba=
d. First Republic=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly9pci5maXJzdHJlcHVibGljLmNvbS9zdGF0aWMtZ=
mlsZXMvMDEzZjU3ZmItYjk4MC00MzUzLWJiYjMtMGU3YTNiMjdmMjBh/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cB662a4292" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> balance sheet</a> shows about $233 billion of asse=
ts, including about $173 billion of loans, but the market value of those as=
sets is considerably lower: Those loans are largely mortgages made at very =
low interest rates, and they have lost a lot of value as rates have gone up=
.. First Republic=C2=A0estimated as of Dec. 31 that its assets were worth a=
bout $27 billion less than their carrying value.<a class=3D"footnote-index"=
 href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display:=
 inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0So figure its assets are worth something like $206 billion on a g=
ood day.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile it=C2=A0has about $105 billion of de=
posits and about $105 billion of secured borrowing from the Federal Reserve=
 and Federal Home Loan Bank system. Of those deposits, roughly $55 billion =
are insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. and roughly $50 billion =
aren=E2=80=99t; $30 billion of the unsecured deposits belong to a consortiu=
m of big banks that=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=
/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGlj=
bGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMTcvYmlnLWJhbmtzLXRydXN0LWZpcnN0LXJlcHVibGljLXdpdGgtdGhlaXI=
tbW9uZXk_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQyNzIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc2=
91cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDI3JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/=
607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbb05a648" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"item=
scope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !im=
portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">deposited money with First Repub=
lic</a> last month to boost confidence. Roughly speaking, the insured depos=
its and the Fed/FHLB ($160 billion total) get paid back first, the uninsure=
d deposits ($50 billion) get paid back next, and everybody else =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0subordinated debt, shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0gets paid back with wh=
atever is left.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So if you can sell the assets for about $210 b=
illion, then the government and all of the depositors get paid back in full=
; if you can=E2=80=99t, they don=E2=80=99t. (Either way, the shareholders a=
re, uh, in trouble.) Again, the assets are worth something like $206 billio=
n, based on First Republic=E2=80=99s filings in December; that would not qu=
ite be enough to pay everyone back. But <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY25iYy5jb20vMjAyMy8wN=
C8yNi9maXJzdC1yZXB1YmxpYy1yZXNjdWUtcGl0Y2gtaGVscC1ub3ctb3ItcGF5LW1vcmUtbGF0=
ZXItd2hlbi1pdC1mYWlscy5odG1s/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5baaa3a6" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> th=
e consensus</a> seems to be that if you actually had to go sell everything =
at once, things would be considerably worse, and there would be a hole of t=
ens of billions of dollars.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so all of the do-something options are bad=
, because of that hole. The most straightforward do-something option is tha=
t the FDIC could seize First Republic, sell its assets, and use the money t=
o pay back depositors. But there would be a hole of tens of billions of dol=
lars. And the FDIC would either have to fill that hole (declaring First Rep=
ublic systemically important and using its deposit insurance fund to pay of=
f the uninsured depositors), or=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>fill that hole (lett=
ing the uninsured depositors bear the loss). The <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9h=
cnRpY2xlcy9maXJzdC1yZXB1YmxpYy1pcy1hLXByb2JsZW0td2l0aC1uby1lYXN5LXNvbHV0aW9=
uLTljMjUyNWI2P21vZD1pdHBfd3NqJnJ1PXlhaG9v/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBca458b9c=
" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;"> Wall Street Journal notes</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The details and extent of the FDIC=E2=80=99s s=
upport will be determined on whether they use the same tool, a so-called sy=
stemic risk exception, that allowed the agency to guarantee all of the depo=
sitors at last month=E2=80=99s two failed institutions.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Invoking that exception again would allow regu=
lators to backstop all of the roughly $50 billion in deposits at First Repu=
blic that are above the FDIC=E2=80=99s insurance limit, including the $30 b=
illion deposited by the big banks.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If the FDIC doesn=E2=80=99t make those deposit=
ors whole, it could reignite questions about such deposits at other regiona=
l banks, causing customers to yank their deposits from smaller firms. But i=
f it does, the FDIC could be accused of bailing out Wall Street.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If the FDIC takes over First Republic at a los=
s, somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0the uninsured depositors (meaning largely but no=
t exclusively the big banks) or the FDIC (also meaning largely the big bank=
s, who <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.25=
7435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMj=
YvZmlyc3QtcmVwdWJsaWMtY2FsbHMtaW4tYS1mYXZvcj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTV=
FVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0=
MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5ae99eeb" item=
prop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color=
: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bord=
er-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #cc=
c;">pay to fund the FDIC=E2=80=99s insurance fund</a>) =E2=80=94=C2=A0has t=
o bear the loss.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are other do-something options that coul=
d happen in the shadow of an FDIC takeover: Another bank could buy First Re=
public and assume its deposits, or other banks could buy its assets at abov=
e-market prices, or banks or private equity firms could buy some equity in =
First Republic. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31=
304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtM=
DQtMjcvZmlyc3QtcmVwdWJsaWMtc3R1Y2staW4tc3RhbmRvZmYtYmV0d2Vlbi11cy1hbmQtYmFu=
a2luZy1pbmR1c3RyeT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWl=
sJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbm=
V5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbe483ca6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsco=
pe=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Bloomberg News reports=
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A number of rescue proposals have so far faile=
d to come to fruition.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Earlier this week, Bloomberg reported that Fir=
st Republic was looking to potentially sell $50 billion to $100 billion of =
assets to big banks that would also receive warrants or preferred equity as=
 an incentive to buy the holdings above their market value.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By Wednesday, the firm=E2=80=99s advisers were=
 privately pitching a similar concept, in which stronger banks would buy bo=
nds off of First Republic=E2=80=99s books for more than they were worth so =
that it could sell shares to new investors. While that would mean booking i=
nitial losses, banks could hold the debts through repayment to be made whol=
e.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But all of these have the same basic outcome, =
which is that somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0probably, again, one or more big bank=
s =E2=80=94=C2=A0steps in to bear the losses, to buy First Republic=E2=80=
=99s assets for more than they are worth. Nobody likes it:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The fate of First Republic Bank has become a g=
ame of chicken between the US government and the lender=E2=80=99s largest r=
ivals, with both sides seeking to avoid steep losses and hoping the other w=
ill handle the troubled firm. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Executives at five of the biggest banks, speak=
ing on the condition they not be named, dismissed the notion of once again =
banding together to prop up First Republic, especially when it could mean p=
aving the way for investors or a competitor to scoop up the firm at a barga=
in price.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If the big banks bear the losses on First Repu=
blic, then whoever ends up owning First Republic =E2=80=94=C2=A0its current=
 shareholders, a new buyer =E2=80=94=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t. You can finesse th=
at a little bit with warrants =E2=80=94=C2=A0effectively, you make the bank=
s who take the losses=C2=A0<em>also=C2=A0</em>the new owners of First Repub=
lic =E2=80=94=C2=A0but the main problem doesn=E2=80=99t go away. The main p=
roblem is the losses.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other option is =E2=80=9Cdo nothing.=E2=80=
=9D First Republic <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly9pci5maXJzdHJlcHVibGljLmNvbS9zdGF0aWMtZmlsZXMvMD=
EzZjU3ZmItYjk4MC00MzUzLWJiYjMtMGU3YTNiMjdmMjBh/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC662=
a4292" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;"> reported earnings</a> on Monday, and they were <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMjcvZmlyc3QtcmVwdWJsaWMtc=
3R1Y2staW4tc3RhbmRvZmYtYmV0d2Vlbi11cy1hbmQtYmFua2luZy1pbmR1c3RyeT9jbXBpZD1C=
QkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHR=
lciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cCbe483ca6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;">  legendarily awful</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Across the industry, First Republi=
c=E2=80=99s quarterly earnings report on Monday has come to be regarded as =
a disaster. The firm announced a larger-than-expected drop in deposits, the=
n declined to take questions as executives presented a 12-minute briefing o=
n results.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But First Republic=C2=A0<em>reported a profit<=
/em>. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392=
.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQ=
tMjUvbWF0dC1sZXZpbmUtcy1tb25leS1zdHVmZi1jcmVkaXQtc3Vpc3NlLXMtZ3JlYXQtYXdmdW=
wtcXVhcnRlcj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0b=
V9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1=
ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4ae6a1b7" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"=
itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  problem</a>, for First Rep=
ublic, is that lots of its low-interest deposits have fled, and it has had =
to replace their funding by borrowing from the Fed, the FHLB and the big ba=
nks at much higher rates. Meanwhile it still has lots of long-term loans ma=
de at low interest rates. If you borrow short at 0% to lend long at 3%, and=
 then your short-term borrowing costs go up to 5% while your loans stay the=
 same, you will be losing 2% a year on your loans, and that is roughly the =
state that First Republic finds itself in. But it is=C2=A0not=C2=A0<em>exac=
tly=C2=A0</em>the state that First Republic finds itself in: It still has s=
ome cheap insured deposits, some short-term assets, some floating-rate asse=
ts, some fee income, and in fact it has managed to scrape out a profit even=
 as rates have moved against it. Can that last? I mean, <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqL=
mNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9maXJzdC1yZXB1YmxpYy1pcy1hLXByb2JsZW0td2l0aC1uby1lYXN5LXNv=
bHV0aW9uLTljMjUyNWI2P21vZD1pdHBfd3NqJnJ1PXlhaG9v/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCc=
a458b9c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> maybe not</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The deposit run has forced First Republic to r=
ely on other, more expensive funding. That makes it hard to generate intere=
st income, and at some point it might not be able to.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThey=E2=80=99ve never been super prof=
itable,=E2=80=9D said Tim Coffey, managing director and analyst at Janney M=
ontgomery Scott. =E2=80=9CNow you=E2=80=99re not growing and you=E2=80=99re=
 layering on really high borrowing and funding costs.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But a bank can stay in business even with some=
 quarterly losses, as long as it remains well capitalized, and as a technic=
al matter First Republic has enough capital to withstand some unprofitable =
quarters. And if you muddle along for long enough, the situation can right =
itself: The long-term low-interest loans will roll off and be replaced with=
 higher-interest new loans, and First Republic=E2=80=99s interest margins w=
ill start to expand again. It might work! If you are a First Republic=C2=A0=
<em>shareholder</em>, =E2=80=9Cdo nothing and hope the business recovers=E2=
=80=9D is clearly the best option.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of course deposits might keep flowing out, but=
 so what? First Republic is now funded in large part with loans from the Fe=
d and the FHLB, and I suppose they could just lend it some more money. When=
 Silicon Valley Bank failed, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2Fy=
dGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMTMvc3ZiLWNvdWxkbi10LWlnbm9yZS1pdHMtbG9zc2VzLWJ1dC10aGU=
tZmVkLWNhbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV=
9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1Z=
mY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB87f9db10" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"i=
temscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none =
!important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-=
bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  the Fed put in place</a> a =
new Bank Term Funding Program that was designed for more or less this purpo=
se: The BTFP lets banks borrow against their assets without taking into acc=
ount interest-rate losses, so that they can replace fleeing deposits with l=
oans from the Fed. US regional banks spent years in a low interest rate env=
ironment, they were caught out by a rapid rate hiking cycle, and the Fed re=
sponded to that problem by lending them money to smooth out the transition.=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The advantage of doing nothing is that nobody =
has to take any losses now. But the regulators seem to want to move. Bloomb=
erg again:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The clock for striking such a deal began ticki=
ng louder late last week. US regulators reached out to some industry leader=
s, encouraging them to make a renewed push to find a private solution to sh=
ore up First Republic=E2=80=99s balance sheet, according to people with kno=
wledge of the discussions.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The calls also came with a warning that banks =
should be prepared in case something happens soon.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And one way for something to happen soon is if=
 the Fed <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.=
257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMjYv=
Zmlyc3QtcmVwdWJsaWMtc2FpZC10by1mYWNlLXBvdGVudGlhbC1jdXJiLW9uLWJvcnJvd2luZy1=
mcm9tLWZlZD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV=
9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1Z=
mY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbb4713a6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"i=
temscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none =
!important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-=
bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  stops lending to First Repu=
blic</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As weeks keep passing without a tr=
ansaction, senior [FDIC] officials are increasingly weighing whether to dow=
ngrade their scoring of the firm=E2=80=99s condition, including its so-call=
ed Camels rating, according to people with direct knowledge of the talks. T=
hat would likely limit the bank=E2=80=99s use of the Fed=E2=80=99s discount=
 window and an emergency facility launched last month, the people said.</p>=
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Why? Why close a bank and take billions of dol=
lars of losses if you don=E2=80=99t have to? The consequences of doing some=
thing are obvious and bad; the consequences of doing nothing are a bit more=
 diffuse.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But let=E2=80=99s talk about some of them. One=
 is that there are legal limits on the Fed=E2=80=99s ability to keep proppi=
ng up First Republic.=C2=A0I mentioned the BTFP, the Fed=E2=80=99s post-Sil=
icon Valley Bank program that lends to banks at 100% of the=C2=A0<em>face v=
alue=C2=A0</em>of their collateral, even if that collateral has lost money =
due to rising interest rates. But only US Treasury and agency securities ar=
e <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZXJhbHJlc2VydmUuZ292L2ZpbmFuY2lhbC1zdGFiaWxpdHkvYmFuay1=
0ZXJtLWZ1bmRpbmctcHJvZ3JhbS5odG0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB684b71cd" itempro=
p=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
> eligible to be BTFP collateral</a>, and First Republic=E2=80=99s assets a=
re mostly loans. Those loans tend to be pretty=C2=A0<em>safe=C2=A0</em>=E2=
=80=94=C2=A0they are mostly <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2Fyd=
GljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMTcvdGhlLXNlYy1jb21lcy1mb3ItYml0dHJleD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDI3=
MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1=
fdGVybT0yMzA0MjcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc=
fb18a71" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;">  mortgages to rich people</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0but they are=
 very exposed to interest-rate risk, so they have lost a lot of value. And =
it can=E2=80=99t use them to borrow from the BTFP.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile these loans <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZXJhbH=
Jlc2VydmUuZ292L3JlZ3JlZm9ybS9kaXNjb3VudC13aW5kb3cuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB98991fbd" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">are eligible collateral</a> at the Fed=E2=80=99s dis=
count window, its more standard lending program, but the discount window <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cuZnJiZGlzY291bnR3aW5kb3cub3JnL1BhZ2VzL0NvbGxhdGVyYWwvY29sbGF0ZXJ=
hbF92YWx1YXRpb24/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0e48d228" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" =
itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">lends against</=
a> the=C2=A0<em>market=C2=A0value </em>of collateral, and these loans have =
lost a lot of value. If deposits keep fleeing from First Republic, its abil=
ity to replace those deposits with Fed loans depends on the=C2=A0<em>market=
=C2=A0</em>value of its assets, which means it might run out of capacity. I=
f the FDIC is worried about that happening sometime soon, then there is som=
e urgency to do something first.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">More generally, the=C2=A0theory of central ban=
king is that central banks should lend to solvent banks, but not prop up in=
solvent banks. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZXJhbHJlc2VydmUuZ292L2Fib3V0dGhlZmVkL3=
NlY3Rpb24xMGIuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfa2336a5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink=
" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Fed=E2=80=99s=
 statutes</a> limit its ability to lend to undercapitalized banks. In some =
obvious economic sense, First Republic is undercapitalized =E2=80=94=C2=A0i=
ts assets are worth less than its liabilities, which is why we are talking =
about this =E2=80=94=C2=A0but legally it is fine and has plenty of regulato=
ry capital.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But at some point, if the regulators conclude =
that First Republic is not viable, it is at least, like, embarrassing for t=
hem to keep lending it money. In the limit case, if all of First Republic=
=E2=80=99s deposits fled, you could imagine the Fed lending it $210 billion=
 (up from its current $105 billion of Fed/FHLB money) so it could continue =
to limp along. But that=E2=80=99s bad! You don=E2=80=99t want a bank out th=
ere doing business, making loans, paying executive salaries, that is entire=
ly funded by the Fed. You need some private-sector endorsement of the bank =
for the Fed to keep supporting it.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also: The losses have already happened. First =
Republic made loans at low interest rates, now interest rates are higher, a=
nd so its loans are not worth what they used to be. As an accounting matter=
, those losses don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>have=C2=A0</em>to be recognized yet; =
First Republic=E2=80=99s balance sheet is still technically solvent, and it=
 can muddle along for a while. But=C2=A0 economically the difference betwee=
n =E2=80=9Cthe banking system reports billions of dollars of losses today a=
nd then normal profits afterwards=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cthe banking system =
bleeds these losses into lower accounting profits for the next few years=E2=
=80=9D is not that great, and the former is more clarifying.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

5px;">Regulatory arbitrage</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I used to be a corporate equity derivatives in=
vestment banker, which means that I=E2=80=99d go to companies and try to co=
nvince them to buy or sell options on their own stock. If you read a financ=
e textbook, you will get the impression that derivatives are mostly about r=
isk management, about hedging and speculation: People who own stock buy put=
 options to protect themselves agains price declines, or sell call options =
to transform some of their potential upside into cash now; people who don=
=E2=80=99t own stock buy calls to take some cheap stock-price risk, etc.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This describes almost none of what I was selli=
ng to these companies. What I was selling, much of the time, was=C2=A0<em>t=
ax deductions: </em>We could build you a derivative that, sure, had the eco=
nomic properties of some call options, but that took advantage of technical=
 tax rules to get you extra deductions.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"=
#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: n=
one !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-b=
lock;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Other times I was selling=C2=A0<em>accounting treatment:=C2=A0</e=
m>We could do a thing that had the economic properties of buying back stock=
, but that took advantage of technical accounting rules to juice your earni=
ngs per share a bit more.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important=
; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> Or I might be=C2=A0selling=C2=A0something like securities-law complian=
ce: If you had some legal restriction on your ability to buy or sell stock,=
 you could buy a derivative from us that was economically like buying or se=
lling stock, but that avoided those restrictions.<a class=3D"footnote-index=
" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display=
: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In my line of work, derivatives were essential=
ly about=C2=A0<em>regulatory arbitrage:=C2=A0</em>There were some complicat=
ed rules, created by legislators or regulators or accounting standards-sett=
ers, and those rules had the effect of rewarding some things and penalizing=
 other things. And our job was to find a way to take something that the rul=
es penalized and=C2=A0<em>turn it into something that the rules rewarded</e=
m>, without changing its economic substance too much.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A famous example. The US tax code=C2=A0discour=
ages short-term trading and encourages long-term investing. If you=C2=A0bor=
row a lot of money to rapidly trade stocks, you will be penalized, paying s=
hort-term capital gains rates. If you buy a long-term call option on a vari=
able basket of stocks that you have management rights over, you will be rew=
arded, paying long-term capital gains rates. If you notice that <em>those t=
wo things are the same</em>, you can have a lucrative career as a derivativ=
es structurer.=C2=A0(And get in trouble: This is <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJn=
LmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTQtMDctMjIvc2VuYXRlLWxpdGVyYXJ5LWNyaXRpY3M=
tZG9uLXQtbGlrZS1maWN0aW9uYWwtZGVyaXZhdGl2ZXM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQyNzIzX01PTkVZU1=
RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwN=
DI3JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB60a98768" item=
prop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color=
: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bord=
er-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #cc=
c;"> the Renaissance Technologies tax trade</a>, and it <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31304392.257435/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0a=
W1lcy5jb20vMjAyMS8wOS8wMi9idXNpbmVzcy9yZW5haXNzYW5jZS1pcnMtcm9iZXJ0LW1lcmNl=
ci1qYW1lcy1zaW1vbnMuaHRtbA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1358bf57" itemprop=3D"S=
toryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> ende=
d up not working</a>.)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The lesson that I learned from my career as a =
derivatives structurer=C2=A0is that much of finance is about this sort of r=
egulatory arbitrage. Economic life is socially constructed, society has rul=
es, and you can make use of the rules to make money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sometimes the rules change and particular busi=
nesses get harder. But there is a sort of conservation law at work; the rul=
es for complex systems have to be somewhat complex, and if you have mastere=
d the current complexities you can probably figure out how to make money of=
f=C2=A0new and different complexities. Tax trades sometimes get shut down b=
y new tax rules, but that doesn=E2=80=99t put tax structurers=C2=A0out of w=
ork; they=E2=80=99re the ones who are best positioned to figure out the new=
 tax trades enabled by the new rules.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sometimes some whole new area of economic life=
 gets brought into the domain of rules, and a new industry springs up to ga=
me it. In my lifetime, environmental, social and governance investing went =
from an academic idea to a huge business with all sorts of competing and ec=
onomically important regulatory and accounting regimes, and so there are lo=
ts of people in the financial industry working on ESG arbitrage.<a class=3D=
"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorat=
ion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00000=
0; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !imp=
ortant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=9CESG Consultant But Evil,=E2=80=9D I <a href=3D"https://l=

irectly for the amount of insurance that you want: If you have $250,000=C2=
=A0in your bank account, it is all insured without you doing anything; if y=
ou have $2,500,000, it mostly isn=E2=80=99t, and that=E2=80=99s just that. =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And that really exists and is a business (=E2=

ately, or whatever =E2=80=94=C2=A0then probably the intermediaries who curr=

to comply with the new rules; they are the experts at FDIC insurance cap st=
ructuring.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a story that you could tell about cryp=

=80=99d be sad. Crypto found a solution to this. The solution was that cryp=
to platforms =E2=80=94=C2=A0exchanges, lending programs, etc. =E2=80=94 wou=
ld take your money and lend it to absolutely wild leveraged degenerate cryp=
to traders, who would pay high interest rates on that money so they could g=
amble with it.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"col=

<span>[6]</span>

h-looking crypto accounts. And then this system, which was as dumb as it so=
unds, collapsed, and crypto platforms like Celsius and Voyager and BlockFi =
went bankrupt.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This system collapsed for a number of reasons,=

s, which reduced the value of speculative assets like crypto. But there is =
a bright side to that, for crypto, which is that now if you are a crypto pl=
atform and you want to attract deposits by promising to pay interest, that =
is much easier. You can take your customers=E2=80=99 money and buy Treasury=
 bills yielding 5% and pay them 4% and keep the difference, instead of taki=
ng your customers=E2=80=99 money and flinging it into crazy leveraged gambl=
ing and losing it and going bankrupt. This is not, I suppose, =E2=80=9Ccryp=

p>
